questions.text: The maximum horizontal range of a projectile is 400 m. The maximum height attained by it will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The following is the solution: To find the maximum height attained by a projectile, we can use the fact that the horizontal range and the maximum height are related by certain mathematical relationships. Let's denote the maximum height as "H" and the horizontal range as "R." The formula for the horizontal range of a projectile launched at an angle θ with an initial velocity v is given by: R = (v2 sin(2θ)) / g where: v = initial velocity of the projectile θ = launch angle (angle of projection) g = acceleration due to gravity (approximately 9.81 m/s²) In this case, we are given that the maximum horizontal range (R) is 400 m. Since the horizontal range is maximized when the launch angle θ is 45 degrees, we can use this information to solve for the initial velocity (v). 400 = (v2 sin(90°)) / g sin(90°) = 1, so the equation simplifies to: 400 = (v2) / g Now, we can solve for v: v2 = 400 g v2 = 400 x 9.81 v2 = 3924.4 v ≈ √3924.4 v ≈ 62.62 m/s Now that we know the initial velocity (v), we can find the maximum height (H) using the following formula: H = (v2 sin²(θ)) / (2 g) For maximum height, θ = 45° (launch angle that maximizes the range). H = (62.622 sin²(45°)) / (2 * 9.81) H = (3924.4 x 0.5) / 19.62 H ≈ 100 meters So, the maximum height attained by the projectile will be approximately 100 meters.
Incorrect as per the formula

questions.text: A projectile is fired at 30o with momentum p. Neglecting friction, the change in kinetic energy when it returns to the ground will be:

options.explanation: According to the law of conservation of energy, projectiles acquire the same kinetic energy when they come at the same level in height. During the projectile, as there is no air resistance, and only conservative gravitational force is acting, the energy remains conserved. Hence Initial total energy equals the final total energy. Since total energy will also be the same, its change in kinetic energy will also be zero i.e. ΔK=0.

questions.text: A passenger is walking on a walkalator (horizontal escalator) at a speed of 6 km/hr relative to the escalator. He starts from point P on the ground and reaches point Q on the ground. The walkalator is moving at 3 km/hr relative to the ground in the direction of point Q. The distance PQ is 120 m. The time taken by him to reach point Q is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per the formula

The following is the solution: Since the passenger is walking on a moving walkalator we have to consider the concept of relative motion. Since the passenger and the walkalator are moving in the same direction, the relative speed will be the sum of the speeds of the passenger and the walkalator. Speed of passenger wrt ground = (speed of passenger wrt walkalator) + (speed of walkalator) = 6+3 = 9 km/hr Converting the speed into m/s we get 9 x 5/18 =2.5 m/s Hence, time taken to cover 120m = 120/2.5 = 48s

questions.explanation: Explanation: What Is Projectile Motion? When a particle is thrown obliquely near the earth’s surface, it moves along a curved path under constant acceleration directed towards the centre of the earth (we assume that the particle remains close to the earth’s surface). The path of such a particle is called a projectile, and the motion is called projectile motion. In a Projectile Motion, there are two simultaneous independent rectilinear motions: Along the x-axis: uniform velocity, responsible for the horizontal (forward) motion of the particle. Along the y-axis: uniform acceleration, responsible for the vertical (downwards) motion of the particle. Parabolic motion is also known as the projectile motion.
